quote: I just wanna make sure he doesn't rob all the way out here and you know?
stewart_read: Gregory's 'rob all the way out here' is a guilt-as-objection signal — he is framing the cancellation as a burden he is trying to spare Ion. This is a trust gift: Gregory is considerate, not resistant. Randy acknowledged it with 'Yeah. Sure. Definitely.' but did not use it as a rapport anchor. A brief 'We really appreciate you calling in — that tells me a lot about you' would have converted the guilt signal into a relationship moment before the rebook ask.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy's brief acknowledgment was sufficient for a logistics call and extending it would have been unnecessary rapport padding.
coaching_implication: Show Randy this moment. The customer handed him a warmth signal and Randy let it pass. On a rebook call where the only asset is goodwill, this is the one place to spend 10 seconds. Suggested rephrase: 'We really appreciate you calling in — most people don't bother. Let's get you set up so it works for your schedule.'

quote: they can call me back tomorrow if they want, and I can check my schedule to see if
stewart_read: Gregory's first statement of the scheduling condition. He is not refusing to rebook — he is telling Randy the correct path: call tomorrow, confirm Friday schedule, then lock the time. Randy did not hear it. Strategic read: Randy could have been setting up a Tuesday anchor while planning a Friday confirmation call. Reactive read: Randy simply did not process the condition and moved to a fixed booking. The evidence supports reactive — there is no follow-up confirmation step offered, no Friday callback mentioned, and the close is a passive escape hatch.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy's Tuesday proposal was a deliberate anchor to give Gregory a concrete slot to hold mentally, with the implicit understanding that it could shift.
coaching_implication: This is the call's primary coaching beat. Walk Randy through the soft_outcome_distinction: 'We can try Tuesday' is not a sit. The correct move was to acknowledge the Friday condition and offer a locked Friday-afternoon callback: 'Got it — let me call you Friday afternoon once your schedule is set and we'll lock it in. Does that work?' That call has a confirmed time; Tuesday has a guess.

quote: I hate to set it up until they set the schedule on Friday, to make sure I'm gonna be off.
stewart_read: Gregory restates the scheduling condition six seconds after the first mention — he is not being difficult, he is being clear. Randy's response ('I'm just gonna shoot for next Tuesday same time?') treats this as resolved when it is not. The word 'shoot' signals Randy himself is uncertain. This is the second touch of the scheduling-uncertainty pattern and the cleaner of the two for coaching because Gregory's language is unambiguous.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 01:35 moment in the 1-on-1. Gregory said it twice. Ask Randy: 'What did Gregory tell you about his schedule?' If Randy can't recall the Friday condition, that's the listening gap. If he recalls it but booked anyway, that's the judgment gap. Different coaching paths.

quote: I'm just gonna shoot for next Tuesday same time?
stewart_read: Randy completes one of six button_up elements — a partial datetime confirmation ('next Tuesday same time') — but skips the remaining five: one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving/at-computer anchor, 'Is that fair?' handshake, and text-followup promise. On a rebook call the full protocol still applies; the no-show risk is higher on a rebook than on a fresh booking because the customer has already cancelled once.
alternative_hypothesis: On a short administrative rebook, a stripped-down confirmation is proportionate and the full button-up would feel bureaucratic.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis has surface appeal but the codex is clear: 'Is that fair?' and the no-driving/at-computer anchor are the two elements most predictive of show rate. Randy could have added both in under 20 seconds. Show him the button_up checklist and ask him to identify what he hit and what he missed. The goal is not to recite the script — it is to understand why each element exists.

quote: Awesome. Well, I'll just get you rescheduled for next Tuesday at the same time. And, you know, if anything changes, you can just let us know and, we can reschedule for you.
stewart_read: Randy closes with a passive escape hatch. 'If anything changes, you can just let us know' is the opposite of a commitment anchor — it explicitly gives Gregory permission to cancel again without friction. The codex's 'Is that fair?' handshake exists precisely to create a micro-commitment that raises the psychological cost of no-showing. Randy replaced the handshake with a cancellation invitation.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have been trying to reduce Gregory's anxiety about the uncertain schedule by signaling flexibility. Given Gregory's twice-stated scheduling uncertainty, some flexibility framing is appropriate.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis is partially valid — some flexibility language is right here. But the balance is wrong. Suggested rephrase direction: lead with the commitment anchor ('Does next Tuesday at 2:30 work for you — is that fair?'), then add the flexibility as a secondary note ('And if your schedule shifts before Friday, just give us a call'). Commitment first, escape hatch second.
